summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROCESS AND PROCEDURE – COSTS – Where the Applicant seeks the costs expended in relation to one discrete issue determined in financial proceedings – Where the Applicant seeks that such costs are paid on an indemnity basis – Where s 114UB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) considered – Where in the substantive proceedings significant adverse credibility findings were made against the Respondent to the costs application – Where neither party was wholly unsuccessful in the substantive proceedings – Where the Respondent being unsuccessful in relation to the discrete issue considered under ss 114UB(3)(c) and 114UB(3)(g) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) – Where offers of settlement made by each party and the terms of such offers considered – Where it is just to make a costs order in favour of the Applicant – Where the Respondent is ordered to pay the Applicant’s costs in a fixed sum

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BEHRENS J

BACKGROUND

These Reasons deal with an application for costs by way of an Amended Application in a Proceeding filed by Mr Yuna on 13 January 2026 (“the costs application”). Mr Yuna was the respondent to a Further Amended Initiating Application filed by Ms Ping on 4 March 2025, by which she instituted financial proceedings seeking orders under Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The proceedings were heard by me over four days in July 2025, last submissions were received on 12 August 2025, and my Reasons for Judgment were delivered on 4 November 2025 (Ping & Yuna [2025] FedCFamC1F 776) (“Reasons”).
Mr Yuna now seeks that Ms Ping pays his costs of the proceedings in the fixed sum of $98,861.99 (“the fixed sum”), “or such other sum as the Court considers appropriate”. By her Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 27 January 2026, Ms Ping seeks that Mr Yuna’s costs application be dismissed.
On behalf of Mr Yuna, the fixed sum is said to have been calculated on the basis that it represents an indemnity to Mr Yuna for the costs of litigating a particular aspect of the financial proceedings – namely, the treatment of assets held within the L Family Trust (“the Family Trust”) which I found to be within the control of Ms Ping and property of the parties to be brought into account in that way in the adjustment of the parties’ property interests. It was said on behalf of Mr Yuna that he was wholly successful in relation to the Family Trust issue. I made some significant adverse credibility findings against Ms Ping in respect of that and other issues.
For the reasons set out below, I order that Ms Ping pay Mr Yuna’s costs fixed in the sum of $74,000.

MATERIAL CONSIDERED

In respect of the costs application, the following was in evidence:
An affidavit of Mr Yuna filed 13 January 2026, with its annexures;
An affidavit of Ms Ping filed 27 January 2026, with its annexures.
I did not make an order for the filing of an affidavit in reply and so I have not received into evidence the affidavit of Mr Yuna filed 3 February 2026.
Mr Yuna filed written submissions on 3 February 2026. Ms Ping filed written submissions on 10 February 2026. Mr Yuna filed brief written submissions in reply on 17 February 2026. Ms Ping’s written submissions attached some messages, which I assume it was intended I receive into evidence. Those messages were not properly put into evidence and, as I set out below, they were irrelevant to the decision I must make. I have not received them into evidence.
Given directions I made on 3 December 2025, and that neither party sought to be heard beyond their written submissions, I have dealt with the matter on the papers.

PROCEDURAL MATTERS

Mr Yuna filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking costs on 2 December 2025 (“the original application”). The original application complied with the requirement, which is now contained in s 114UB(4)(b) of the Act, that a party to the proceedings may make an application for costs “within 28 days after the final order in the proceedings is made”.
I reject the submissions made on behalf of Ms Ping to the effect that:
The original application was not a valid application for costs;
Mr Yuna required leave to file out of time (and/or to file an amended application); and
that I had effectively granted such leave without according procedural fairness when I made procedural orders on 3 December 2025.
I am satisfied that, notwithstanding the only evidence in support of the original application was an affidavit from his new lawyers to the effect that they had only obtained his file from his previous lawyers the day prior, the original application was a valid application for costs and filed within time. Mr Yuna was not required to apply for leave to file an application out of time. Indeed, given the requirement to file within 28 days after the making of a final order is now in the Act and not in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) (which can be dispensed with), it seems that there is no longer any power to grant such leave. I made procedural orders on 3 December 2025 providing for Mr Yuna to file an amended Application in a Proceeding and further evidence. He was, in any case, entitled to file an amended Application in a Proceeding at or before the first court date (r 2.50(1)(b)(i)). My directions provided for a timetable for the filing of and responding to material by each Mr Yuna and Ms Ping. Those directions were complied with. There is no basis for complaint in this process – in particular, there has been no denial of procedural fairness. No issue of prejudice arises.

COSTS ORDERS – RELEVANT LAW

Section 114UB of the Act governs the making of costs orders. The usual rule in family law proceedings is that each party bears their own costs. The section provides as follows:
114UB Costs orders—general

(1) Subject to subsection (2) of this section, subsection 102QAB(6) and sections 114UD and 114UE, each party to proceedings under this Act is to bear the party’s own costs.

(2) If, in proceedings under this Act, the court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the court may, subject to subsection (3) of this section, sections 114UC and 114UD and the applicable Rules of Court, make any order for costs and security for costs that the court considers just, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise.

...

(3) In considering what costs order (if any) should be made, the court must have regard to the following:
(a) the financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings;

(b) whether any party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings and, if so, the terms of the grant of the assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure under subsection 71B(1), 90RI(1) or 90YJA(1);

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer;

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.

...
(5) The court may order that a party is entitled to costs:
(a) of a specific amount; or

(b) as assessed on a particular basis (for example, party and party, solicitor and client or indemnity); or

(c) to be calculated in accordance with the method stated in the order; or

(d) for part of the proceedings, or part of an amount assessed in accordance with the applicable Rules of Court.
(6) If the court makes a costs order but does not specify the method for the calculation of the costs, the costs are to be assessed on a party and party basis.

(7) Subject to subsection (3), the court may make a costs order in favour of, or against, a party to the proceedings regardless of the degree to which the party has been successful in the proceedings.
In Penfold & Penfold (1980) 144 CLR 311, when considering the provision of the Act which was at that time in force in relation to costs and which was for all relevant purposes identical to the current provision, the High Court considered that, while there must be justifying circumstances for a costs order to be made, no “additional or special onus” is placed upon applicants in their pursuit of costs. Once justifying circumstances are found, the Court has discretion to award costs. In this matter, it is pertinent to note that costs orders are not made for the purpose of punishing a litigant; they are compensatory in nature: Northern Territory v Sangare [2019] HCA 25; (2019) 265 CLR 164 (“Northern Territory v Sangare”) at [25]; Ohn v Walton (1995) 36 NSWLR 77 at 79; Allplastics Engineering Pty Ltd v Dornoch Ltd [2006] NSWCA 33 at [34]. A party’s conduct in relation to the proceedings is, however, a matter to which I must have regard pursuant to s 114UB(3)(c) because such behaviour may have inappropriately increased the costs incurred by the other party. I reject the submission on behalf of Mr Yuna to the effect that a failure to make a costs order in these circumstances would set a “poor example” and send a message to parties in family law proceedings that the fabrication of evidence carries no consequences. I also reject as irrelevant the submission on behalf of Ms Ping that:
An [order] for costs by the Court may encourage the Applicant to treat such an order as punishment of the Respondent and use this as impetus to pursue further litigation in other jurisdictions as is threatened by the Applicant to the Respondent directly and also to the Respondent’s friends and family of friends.
The role of costs orders is compensatory only – they play no broader role.
I need to be satisfied that the conditions of s 114UB(2) have been met. That is, I must be satisfied that there are circumstances that make it just for me to make an order for costs, having regard to the matters in s 114UB(3).

DISPOSITION

I am amply satisfied that it is just for me to make an order for costs in favour of Mr Yuna. In summary of what is said below that is because, as I set out in detail in my Reasons, I am satisfied that Ms Ping concocted evidence in relation to a number of matters – including by giving false evidence designed to persuade the Court that she did not control the Family Trust. She was wholly unsuccessful in persuading the Court that the property held in the Family Trust was not property of the parties. That issue consumed significant time and resources both before and during the final hearing.
I turn now to consider each of the relevant matters, noting that neither party has received any legal aid in respect of the proceedings and so that factor is not relevant, and that there was no evidence that the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the Court.

The financial circumstances of the parties (s 114UB(3)(a))

The property pool which I found was to be divided between the parties was approximately $7,557,673 (to be adjusted for the actual sale prices of the properties, costs of sale, the winding up of the self-managed superannuation fund and other relatively minor matters). Ms Ping is to receive 52.5 per cent of that pool. In the Reasons, I rejected her evidence that the property held in the Family Trust was not in her control and that therefore it should not form part of the pool to be divided between the parties. Accordingly, both Ms Ping and Mr Yuna are entitled to property of significant value.
Other relevant findings I made (and which contributed to my conclusion that Ms Ping should receive an additional 2.5 per cent of the property pool) were:
Both parties are now approaching retirement age;
Ms Ping was let go from her employment in April 2021. She lives with a rare eye condition in both eyes, and since 2020 has been legally blind. She has not been engaged in the paid workforce apart from some casual work in 2023. She is in receipt of a disability pension and receives assistance from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (“NDIS”). From January 2022 until January 2026 (when her plan was due for review), she would have received total funded supports of $96,897.66, including funding to support with household cleaning, cooking and meal preparation. She is unlikely to engage in the paid workforce, and it is reasonable for her not to do so. In addition to support from the NDIS and her family, she will need some paid assistance; and
Mr Yuna continues to operate his business and has, and will continue to have for at least some time, a modest income from that business.
In summary, I found at [122] that:
... Each will receive significant cash payments from the sale proceeds of the properties, and will retain cash in bank accounts. Each will receive some modest superannuation which they will be able to access soon. [Ms Ping] will retain the assets within the Family Trust and will be able to distribute income and/or capital to herself. [Mr Yuna] will receive or retain the entities through which he operates his businesses, and the income they generate. I am satisfied that this outcome represents a just and equitable distribution of assets between the parties. Both parties will be relatively comfortable financially.
There is no evidence that persuades me that anything has changed significantly since the final hearing – save that the parties have paid some of their respective legal fees. Ms Ping reiterates her claim not to have access to the property within the Family Trust. I have already rejected that evidence. Given the recency of the final hearing, and the fact that it was concerned entirely with the parties’ financial circumstances, there was no need for Mr Yuna to put on further evidence as to his financial circumstances, and I reject the submissions on behalf of Ms Ping to the contrary. The findings, summarised above, which I made in relation to the parties’ financial circumstances are apposite.
I do not accept that Ms Ping is in any genuine financial difficulty, nor that she is significantly less well off than Mr Yuna. On her own evidence, she has significant readily available cash. In any case, even impecuniosity is not a bar to the making of a costs order (Nada & Nettle (Costs) [2014] FamCAFC 207; (2014) FLC 93-612; Northern Territory v Sangare).

The conduct of the parties to the proceedings (s 114UB(3)(c))

I made significant adverse credibility findings against Ms Ping in the Reasons, finding she was “a thoroughly unreliable witness” and that there were multiple inconsistencies in her evidence. More specifically, I found as follows:
... I do not accept [Ms Ping’s] nor [Ms O’s] evidence that the funds that a [Ms Ping] already had in her bank account which were applied to the purchase of the warehouses were [Ms O’s]. That evidence was a concoction to try to persuade the Court that none of the parties’ funds had been contributed towards the warehouses (Reasons at [35]).
... I do not accept the frankly incredible evidence of [Ms Ping] and [Ms O] that decisions about distributions were made by [Ms O] and simply implemented by [Ms Ping] (Reasons at [43]).
... I am comfortably satisfied that the deed was produced by [Ms Ping] and [Ms O] during these proceedings and was not signed in 2008 (Reasons at [67]).
I do not accept [Ms Ping’s] evidence that the monies she paid towards the purchase of the warehouse properties were monies which were in fact [Ms O’s] (or owed by her to [Ms O]) or else borrowed by the Family Trust from [Ms O] by way of an investment by [Ms O]. I find that both [Ms Ping’s] and [Ms O’s] calculations and evidence in relation to this aspect of the matter were a concoction, in an attempt to advance [Ms Ping’s] interests in these proceedings ... (Reasons at [40]).
Also relevant is what I am satisfied was Ms Ping’s failure at various stages in the proceedings to comply with her disclosure obligations in relation to the Family Trust – which, from the commencement of proceedings, she maintained and, as I found falsely, was not within her control.
These are matters of conduct which are relevant for me to consider. Each of these findings took a significant amount of cross examination to establish. The factual matters also underpinned legal arguments about whether property held in the Family Trust was property of the parties themselves. Those legal arguments in turn consumed a significant part of the final hearing.
I also made some adverse findings about Mr Yuna, but they related to comparatively minor matters and did not consume the Court’s time significantly.
I do not accept that it is relevant to my decision that Mr Yuna has apparently engaged in some correspondence with family, friends and the family of friends, in which he draws on aspects of my judgment to criticise Ms Ping. Again, the purpose of a costs order is compensatory, not punitive. Restrictions on publication and related criminal offences are dealt with in Pt XIVB of the Act.
Ms Ping complains about various aspects of Mr Yuna’s conduct in relation to interlocutory matters, but in none of those proceedings were Ms Ping’s costs awarded or reserved. On one occasion, her application for costs was dismissed.
I accept that there were issues with Mr Yuna’s own disclosure during the proceedings. I do not accept the submission on behalf of Ms Ping that I “made no findings that [the sisters’ joint account] was a resource nor an asset of [Mr Yuna]”, nor the criticisms which the submissions on behalf of Ms Ping make of Mr Yuna’s approach to this issue. The question of who had made what contributions to the sisters’ joint account was dwarfed by Ms Ping’s position that the joint account was not beneficially hers. As to that issue, I found that the sisters had concocted a Deed of Trust in relation to that account and:
Accordingly, I cannot be satisfied that the beneficial ownership of the funds in the sisters’ joint bank account was other than their legal entitlement to half each. While, following Shinohara, I can no longer “add back” the relevant funds to the pool, the premature distribution of those funds can be taken into account pursuant to s 79(5)(v). I deal with that matter below (Reasons at [68]).

Whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings (s 114UB(3)(e))

Neither party was wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings. Mr Yuna sought a 50/50 division of the parties’ assets, including the add backs for which he contended. At the commencement of the final hearing, Ms Ping sought 70 per cent of a pool excluding Mr Yuna’s add backs and excluding the property held within the Family Trust. Ultimately, I ordered that Ms Ping receive 52.5 per cent of a pool which excluded Mr Yuna’s add backs but included the property held within the Family Trust (with a value of $1,664,111 in a pool of $7,557,673).
Mr Yuna claims that Ms Ping was wholly unsuccessful in relation to the Family Trust issue – that is the main plank of his claim for costs, along with her conduct in relation to that issue.
There is a question as to the appropriateness of dividing up proceedings in this way – and not just because of the difficulty of identifying what costs can be attributed to which issue. It is relatively clear – as Ms Ping submits – that par (e) of s 114UB(3) is concerned only with the situation where a party is wholly unsuccessful in the determination of all issues.
Nonetheless, it is appropriate to have regard to the fact that one party was unsuccessful at least in respect of a major aspect of their case either under par (c) (“conduct of the parties”) or par (g) (“such other matters as the court considers relevant”). It is important, however, if that is done to also have regard to issues where that party has been successful.
The matter of the Family Trust was undoubtedly the major issue during the final hearing, consuming a significant part of the cross examination of Ms Ping, and part of the cross examination of her witnesses. It was addressed at comparative length in both oral and written submissions.
I summarised Ms Ping’s arguments in relation to the Family Trust issue at [20]–[22] of the Reasons:
20 [Mr Yuna] contends that, given the matters set out above, [Ms Ping] controls the Family Trust and that on well-recognised principles, property owned by the Family Trust is the property of the parties. [Ms Ping] contends that the property owned by the Family Trust is, at most, a financial resource in her hands. No orders were made during the preparation of the matter for the filing of Points of Claim and the basis or bases of [Ms Ping’s] position changed at various times during the proceedings.

21 By the time of final oral submissions, [Ms Ping] contended that the Family Trust is not property of the parties on one of three alternate bases:
(1) She holds “her interest” in the Family Trust on trust for her sister [Ms O]; or

(2) The property owned within the Family Trust is not the property of the parties because there are named beneficiaries who are neither parties to the marriage nor children of the marriage and/or because the corpus of the Family Trust does not consist of property acquired by the parties to the marriage; or

(3) The property owned within the Family Trust is not the property of the parties because the units can only be transferred to [Ms Ping] herself or at her direction if the other trustees of the Unit Trust agree.
22 Only point 3 was pursued with any real vigour in final submissions, although the alternate positions were not abandoned.
The third argument was raised for the first time in final submissions. Each of the arguments was virtually doomed to fail. The first was supported by what I found was false evidence, the second was contrary to well-established authority, and the third did not have a sound basis in either the Trust Deed of W Unit Trust, nor in the other evidence.
While it is true that Mr Yuna unsuccessfully contended for add backs in respect of distributions from the Family Trust, that was quite a separate issue from the central question of whether the property held in the Family Trust was property of the parties.
There were other issues where Mr Yuna was unsuccessful – for example, in relation to the value of his business, and in establishing that Ms Ping had some earning capacity notwithstanding that she is legally blind – but they paled into insignificance (both in terms of the time spent on them and their impact on the outcome) compared with the Family Trust issue and Ms Ping’s conduct in respect of that issue.
The decision in Shinohara & Shinohara (2025) FLC 94-266 was handed down during the final hearing, and Mr Yuna could not have been expected to anticipate that his add back arguments would thereby be defeated. In any case, I took those matters into account when determining the just and equitable s 79(5)(v) adjustment.

Whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer (s 114UB(3)(e))

There was some evidence before me about what offers had been made, but it is very difficult to assess those offers against the outcome reached because of the significant differences between the parties as to the pool. It appears uncontested that Ms Ping only made one offer, which occurred on 19 February 2025. Notably, that offer included the property in the Family Trust, but provided for her to receive 72.5 per cent. At final hearing, her senior counsel conceded that an outcome where Ms Ping received 70 per cent (including 15 per cent for s 79(5) factors) was unsustainable. She ultimately received 52.5 per cent of the pool, including the property in the Family Trust.
I accept that Mr Yuna made several attempts to settle the matter, including by making offers that Ms Ping receive a higher percentage of the property pool than he did (on 29 May 2023 he made an informal offer that she receive 60 per cent and he receive 40 percent and on 13 November 2023, he made an offer that she receive 55 per cent). The difficulty is that, in the case of the first offer, it was not clear what pool he contended for and, in the case of the second offer, Mr Yuna included contended add backs (at a greater level even than was contended for at the commencement of the final hearing) and a value for the Family Trust of $2 million. Neither of these offers therefore clearly represent in dollar terms a better outcome than he received.
In the circumstances, it is not possible to conclude that Mr Yuna received a better outcome in the judgment than he would have received had Ms Ping accepted an offer he made, nor that his offers came close to the ultimate outcome. His offered percentage outcome was, however, more generous to Ms Ping than she ultimately received. Ms Ping did not receive a better outcome in the judgment than she would have received if Mr Yuna had accepted an offer she had made, and her proposed percentage outcome was well out of range (as her senior counsel acknowledged in final submissions).

Is a costs order just?

Having regard to all the matters set out above, but most particularly Ms Ping’s conduct and the outcome in respect of the Family Trust issue, I am satisfied that Ms Ping should pay some of Mr Yuna’s costs.

On what basis should the costs be paid?

Given I am satisfied that an order for costs is just, I then need to determine on what basis such costs should be paid. The different bases upon which costs can be ordered are set out in s 114UB(5) (extracted above).
The Rules provide further guidance:
12.17 Method of calculation of costs

(1) The court may order that a party is entitled to costs:
(a) of a specific amount; or

(b) as assessed on a particular basis (for example, party and party, solicitor and client or indemnity); or

(c) to be calculated in accordance with the method stated in the order; or

(d) for part of the proceeding, or part of an amount, assessed in accordance with Schedule 3.
Note: See subsection 114UB(5) of the Family Law Act.

(2) If costs are payable under the Family Law Act or these Rules, or the court orders that costs be paid and does not specify the method for their calculation, the costs are to be assessed on a party and party basis.

Note: See subsection 114UB(6) of the Family Law Act.

(3) In making an order under subrule (1), the court may consider the following:
(a) the importance, complexity or difficulty of the issues;

(b) the reasonableness of each party’s behaviour in the proceeding including by having regard to the matters set out in subrule 12.08(2);

(c) the rates ordinarily payable to lawyers in comparable proceedings;

(d) whether a lawyer’s conduct has been improper, unfair, unreasonable or disproportionate;

(e) the time properly spent on the proceeding, or in complying with pre‑action procedures;

(f) whether expenses (paid or payable) are fair, reasonable and proportionate.
Mr Yuna seeks that his costs in respect of the Family Trust issue be paid on an indemnity basis. In his affidavit filed 13 January 2026, he has set out his evidence about what those costs are, by way of the following:
Narrative evidence about the work that had to be done by his solicitors to address the Family Trust issue, including the issue of subpoenas, correspondence, and the preparation of summaries pursuant to s 50 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth);
In respect of solicitors’ and paralegals’ work addressing the Family Trust issue, a table setting out the date work was undertaken, a description of such work, by whom such work was completed, the monetary sums incurred for the work undertaken and showing a “discount” for work that related to issues other than that of the Family Trust;
A similar table in respect of disbursements;
A similar table in respect of senior counsel’s fees.
He has complied with the Rules by putting in evidence the relevant Costs Agreements. He has also annexed the relevant invoices. There was no standard “costs schedule” setting out costs on the scale.
The principles for the award of costs on an indemnity basis are well established. There must be circumstances of an exceptional kind to warrant such an order (Kohan and Kohan [1992] FamCA 116; (1993) FLC 92-340).
I am satisfied that the circumstances of this litigation – and, most particularly, Ms Ping’s conduct in concocting false evidence in the proceedings, leading to significant expenditure of costs on the Family Trust issue – are exceptional and are such as to warrant an award of costs on more than the usual (party and party) basis.
The Act makes it plain that I can fix an amount for costs. Doing so avoids the necessity for the taxing of costs, which is consistent with the overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions set out in s 95 of the Act. Fixing an amount of costs will bring to an end in a just, quick and efficient way the difficult and long-running proceedings between these parties so that they can move on with their lives. I adopt the approach in Warbrick & Warbrick (No. 2) (2021) FLC 94-030, whereby the Full Court did “not intend to permit the costs question to become, in effect, satellite litigation” and so fixed the costs amount.
I am sufficiently satisfied on the evidence of Mr Yuna that the amount of $98,861.99 represents approximately the amount that he expended on the Family Trust issue. On behalf of Ms Ping, while criticism is made of the lack of visibility in relation to “why selected charges are attributable to the purported [Family Trust] issue” (Written Submissions, paragraph 29.2), Ms Ping has had access to that correspondence and takes no issue with any particular entry in the tables which are in evidence. I am satisfied that the discount of 50 per cent applied to certain charges – including senior counsel’s fees – to reflect the proportion of the charge which is attributable to the Family Trust issue, is a reasonable approximation given the amount of time occupied by the issue at the final hearing and the complexity of that issue as compared with other issues.
An order for costs on or approaching an indemnity basis does not, however, entitle the payee to receive all of what they expended. The Rules make it clear that reasonableness is the guiding principle. The absence of a proper schedule of costs showing what the relevant cost would have been pursuant to the relevant scale of costs (and therefore necessarily including such information as the numbers of words in correspondence) means it is difficult to assess the reasonableness of the costs in fact incurred. No submission was made on behalf of Ms Ping that the costs sought were other than reasonable, although issue was taken with the attribution of the figure sought in relation to the Family Trust issue.
Ultimately, I have determined to make an order for costs to be paid in a fixed sum of $74,000, which represents approximately 75 per cent of the costs which Mr Yuna has expended on this issue. I note that the sum represents approximately 15 per cent of Mr Yuna’s total costs of the proceedings (including interlocutory applications) of $487,693.66. The issue was significantly complex, required the issue of subpoenas and analysis of numerous transactions and documents, and required significant time and skill in cross examination and submissions. Both parties engaged senior counsel, which was appropriate. Ms Ping herself spent over $500,000 in legal costs and so the parties’ legal costs are similar – a relevant factor going to reasonableness.
I am satisfied, doing the best I can with the evidence and having what regard I can to the scale of costs and the matters set out in r 12.17, that the amount of $74,000 is “logical, fair and reasonable” (per Einstein J in Idoport Pty Ltd v National Australia Bank Ltd & Ors [2007] NSWSC 23 at [9], adopted in the family law context by Kent J in Stoian v Fiening (Costs) [2014] FamCA 944 at [91] and McClelland DCJ in Pascoe & Larsen (No. 2) [2022] FedCFamC1A 126 at [28]).
Ms Ping has the funds in a bank account to meet this order, and so she should be required to make the payment within 30 days.
